_meta.purpose: Exam-intelligence knowledge base for an AI tutor 'exam drill' mode.
_meta.corpus: Official past exams + solutions, pdftotext extractions. Semesters: Spring 22, Winter 22-23 ('Winter 23'), Spring 23, Winter 23-24 ('Winter 24'), Spring 24, Winter 24-25 ('Winter 25'), Spring 25 (Mo'ed A/B), Winter 25-26 Mo'ed A (solution only). ~15 individual exam papers.
extraction_caveats: Exam prose is Hebrew (RTL) and garbled by pdftotext; C++/Python code blocks and point values extracted cleanly and are high-confidence.
UML class diagrams are images and did NOT survive extraction in any file; design intent recovered from surrounding prose+code.

Official solution keys contain real transcription/logic bugs (e.g. map::operator[] inside a const method; dynamic_cast on the iterator instead of *it; json.dump argument order swapped; iterating a dict's keys instead of .values()). Solutions below are cleaned/corrected.

topic_frequency.notes: The backbone of Q1 in EVERY exam. Given an abstract base with a pure-virtual method, implement 2-4 concrete subclasses that override it; virtual dispatch is then exercised through base-class pointers/containers. Examples: Contestant/Singer/Dancer, Vehicle/Ship/Airplane, Sailor/Worker/Warrior, Vegetable/Cabbage/Tomato, Cell/EmptyCell/PizzaCell, Animal/Land-WaterAnimal, Channel/IG-WAChannel, Package/Internal-ExternalPackage.

topic_frequency.topic: abstract classes, pure virtual functions & virtual destructor
topic_frequency.notes: Always paired with inheritance. A missing 'virtual ~Base()' is an explicitly penalized common mistake. Signature correctness of the pure virtual (const, params, '= 0', 'override') is graded.

Every Q1 ends with a 'redesign for this new feature, name the design pattern you used' sub-part. Patterns seen across exams: Strategy, Composite, Decorator, Factory, Adapter, Singleton, Command, Prototype/virtual-clone. Students must also justify WHY (and often why a wrong-looking alternative like Decorator/Strategy does NOT fit).

Q2 ('basic C++') is essentially always a generic container or generic algorithm: Matrix<T>, MultiSet<T>, HashSet<T,Hash>, Graph<V>, Tree<T>, MemManager<T>, Poly<T>, tuple<T,N>, Unrolled<T>, CyclicBuffer<T>, cow_ptr<T>, plus STL-style all_of/calcAvg. A recurring sub-part asks 'what operations must type T support?' (copy ctor, assignment, operator==/>/+, default ctor).

Two flavors: (a) IMPLEMENT a deep-copy copy-ctor/assignment/dtor for a class owning a raw T*; (b) THEORY: 'are the compiler-generated copy ctor/assignment/dtor sufficient? explain.' The discriminator is always: raw owning pointer => must write them (double-free/dangling otherwise); only STL/smart-pointer members => defaults suffice.

topic_frequency.topic: dynamic memory: raw new[]/delete[], exception-safe ctor (no STL)
topic_frequency.notes: Q2 frequently forbids STL and smart pointers, forcing raw arrays. Tests: allocation in ctor, cleanup-and-rethrow on bad_alloc, pointer arithmetic for indexing, a leak-free destructor.

topic_frequency.topic: smart pointers & ownership semantics (unique_ptr / shared_ptr / std::move)
topic_frequency.notes: Increasingly dominant in recent exams. unique_ptr for exclusive ownership (captain, actions, cart items) => class becomes non-copyable; shared_ptr for shared/flyweight objects (crew, grid cells, channels). Ownership transfer via std::move, factory functions returning smart pointers. A classic pitfall flagged: make_shared(this).

Nearly universal. Define a custom exception inheriting std::exception, override what(), throw on error conditions (full/empty/not-found/invalid). Also std::invalid_argument, std::out_of_range, std::runtime_error. try/catch used for cleanup and for exception propagation.

operator<< (friend, delegating to a virtual print), operator[] (const AND non-const pair), operator() (call operator / matrix access), operator+ (free function, often delegating to member operator+=), comparison operators, conversion operators (operator std::set<T>(), operator vector<T>()), operator* and operator-> for smart pointers.

topic_frequency.topic: STL containers (map, set, vector, list, queue, pair)
topic_frequency.notes: Allowed in Q1 and part of Q2 unless stated. Container CHOICE is graded: map for unique-key lookup, list for O(1) priority insert, map<T,int> for multiset counts, map<int,set<T>> to group-by. Notes penalize e.g. map<Point,Cell> when Point has no operator<, or map::operator[] inside a const method (use .at()).

Graded pervasively as a 'correct programming principles' item. Pass by const&, const methods, const/non-const overload pairs, returning const& (and NOT returning a dangling reference). A reference cannot be null (so null-checking a const T& is wrong).

Implement a nested iterator class (holding a reference back to its container) with begin()/end(), operator++ (prefix), operator!=, operator* so 'for (auto& x : container)' compiles. Often built on modulo advance (ring buffer) or 'equal elements are consecutive' (multiset).
Q3 staple. with open(...), readlines, os.listdir / os.path.join / isdir / isfile, filter files by extension, stream large files line-by-line, propagate open errors.

topic_frequency.topic: Python: comprehensions replacing loops (no for/while, no recursion)
topic_frequency.notes: A signature constraint of this course. Specific sub-parts forbid loops/recursion and must be solved with list/dict comprehensions. Recurring tricks: sum(list_of_lists, []) to flatten, the two-pass 'build the distinct keys then a nested comprehension per key' group-by idiom, any()/all() over slices.

Define classes parsing a delimited/structured line into typed fields; defensive copies (name[:], .copy()); dunder methods __eq__ (with isinstance guard), __str__, __repr__, __lt__ so objects work in sets/queues/sorting.
json.load / json.dump to read/write records; often combined with the no-loops constraint. (Watch the real solution-key bug: json.dump(obj, file) argument order.)
Final Q3 sub-part: a runnable script guarded by if __name__ == '__main__', reading sys.argv, validating argument count, printing a friendly error to sys.stderr (no raw traceback) and sys.exit(1).
class MyError(Exception): pass, raised on duplicate/missing/invalid records; sometimes re-raising a library error as ValueError.

Every C++ design question opens with a 5-6 pt 'draw the class diagram' and later a second diagram for the redesign. Rewards a minimal design that stays open to extension; penalizes wrong association arrows (e.g. drawing containment where there is none).
Used to discriminate the runtime subtype inside a base-pointer container: prefer a SuperWand, count Warriors on a ship, sort vehicles into airplane/ship fleets.

Rare and light, appears only in some Winter/Spring Mo'ed A Python questions. Tested: git branch/add/commit -m/push sequence; explaining merge-conflict markers (<<<<<<< HEAD / ======= / >>>>>>>); rebase-vs-merge conflict counts; reading ls/cd/pwd output. NOT a heavily weighted topic.
Never a standalone Big-O question, but recent exams grade choosing an efficient data structure/algorithm (O(1) ring-buffer vs O(n) shift; linear two-pointer merge; append is O(1)).

question_archetypes.description: A multi-part (~43-50 pt) problem framed around a themed system (zoo, tea shop, delivery company, library, soccer game, drone fleet, logger). Sub-parts: (a) draw a UML class diagram; (b-c) implement an abstract base hierarchy + a managing container class with custom exceptions; (d) redesign for a new feature and NAME the design pattern; (e) implement the pattern class.
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: First read ALL sub-parts before writing code; the later parts reveal what the base class must expose. Make the base abstract with a virtual destructor and the varying behavior a pure virtual. Pick containers by access pattern (map for by-name lookup). Decide ownership up front (unique_ptr => delete copy ops; shared_ptr => shared lifetime). For the pattern sub-part, match the requirement to the pattern (repeatable/stackable ops=>Command or Composite; per-object swappable behavior=>Strategy; runtime add-on that changes a computed value order-dependently=>Decorator; create-by-name/per-source=>Factory; polymorphic copy=>virtual clone/Prototype; single global=>Singleton; wrap an incompatible existing class=>Adapter) and justify against the obvious wrong alternative.

question_archetypes.archetype: Generic container / algorithm from scratch (Q2, 'basic C++')
question_archetypes.description: A ~27-31 pt problem: implement a class template (Matrix, MultiSet, HashSet, Graph, Tree, Poly, tuple, Unrolled list, CyclicBuffer, cow_ptr) or STL-style generic algorithms (all_of, calcAvg, groupBy). Usually one sub-part fixes the constructor/interface, others add operators, iterators, or copy control, and a final sub-part asks 'what requirements must type T satisfy?'.
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: Check whether STL is allowed: if forbidden you own raw memory and MUST write the Rule of 3 (deep copy, exception-safe ctor, leak-free dtor); if STL members only, the compiler defaults are correct and you should say so. Provide const AND non-const operator[] / operator*. For the 'requirements on T' part, scan your own code for every operation you invoked on a T (default ctor, copy ctor, assignment, ==, >, +, etc.).

question_archetypes.description: A short (2-5 pt) sub-part: 'Does this class need a user-defined copy ctor / assignment / destructor? Is the class copyable? Explain why or why not.'
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: Answer with the discriminator: raw owning pointer => YES, defaults would shallow-copy and cause double-free/dangling (UB). Only STL/smart-pointer members => NO, defaults recurse correctly. A unique_ptr member => class is non-copyable (unique_ptr has no copy ctor) so you should = delete copy ops or provide a deep copy via clone(). A Singleton => = delete copy ops to preserve the single instance.

question_archetypes.description: Given a new requirement, name the pattern that fits and explain why (often ruling out a plausible wrong answer).
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: Map requirement->pattern and always contrast with the trap alternative. E.g. 'apply the same topping multiple times, in any order' = Command (not Decorator/Strategy, which don't naturally reapply the same op). 'A container that is itself a member of the hierarchy and delegates recursively' = Composite. 'Swap one algorithm at runtime' = Strategy. 'Wrap and augment a computed value, order matters' = Decorator. 'Create the right subtype by name/source' = Factory. 'Copy through a base pointer' = virtual clone/Prototype.

question_archetypes.description: Extend a hand-written container with begin()/end() and a nested iterator (operator++, operator!=, operator*) so a range-based for loop works.
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: Remember what 'for (auto x : c)' expands to: auto it=c.begin(); it!=c.end(); ++it; *it. The iterator stores a position plus a reference to its container; operator!= compares position (and container identity); operator* returns the element (const& for read-only). Reuse the container's advance logic (e.g. modulo for ring buffers).

question_archetypes.archetype: Python function under a hard constraint (no loops / no regex / streaming)
question_archetypes.description: Implement a Python function that must avoid for/while (comprehensions only), or must parse without the re/HTML/XML modules, or must stream a file too large for memory.
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: For no-loops: use list/dict comprehensions, sum(lists, []) to flatten, and the two-pass group-by (distinct keys, then a nested comprehension per key). For no-regex parsing: str.split / find / slicing / isdigit / startswith / endswith. For streaming: iterate the file object line by line, never readlines() the whole thing.

question_archetypes.description: Final Q3 sub-part: a command-line script with __name__=='__main__' guard, sys.argv parsing/validation, JSON or file processing, and friendly error reporting to stderr.
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: Guard with if __name__ == '__main__'; validate len(sys.argv); wrap the work in try/except that prints a human message to sys.stderr (NOT a traceback) and sys.exit(1). Let lower-level functions propagate exceptions upward.

question_archetypes.description: A small (2-4 pt) conceptual item: give the git command sequence to commit & push a branch, or explain a merge conflict and its markers, or contrast rebase vs merge.
question_archetypes.how_to_approach: Memorize the core sequence (git add . ; git commit -m '...' ; git push origin <branch>) and the conflict markers (<<<<<<< HEAD / ======= / >>>>>>>). Low effort, high certainty points.

typical_sections: Q1 (~43-50 pts): C++ object-oriented DESIGN + implementation of a themed system (UML, abstract hierarchy, managing container, custom exceptions, a design pattern).
Q2 (~27-31 pts): 'Basic'/fundamentals C++ = a generic container or generic algorithm (templates, Rule of 3 or smart pointers, operator overloading, sometimes an iterator).
Q3 (~20-26 pts): Python (classes, file/JSON I/O, comprehensions, a runnable script); in some Mo'ed-A sittings the last 2-6 pts are Git/Unix.
exam_structure.format_facts: 3 questions, 100 points, 3-hour time limit, open-book (any printed/handwritten material; no electronics). Nearly 100% code-writing/design plus short 'explain why' theory. NO multiple choice, NO find-the-bug, NO standalone output-prediction (expected-output comments appear only as a spec to implement against). STL and 'using namespace std;' and all #includes are assumed unless a question forbids STL. Reserve-duty (miluim) students may substitute a homework grade for the Python question / a specific sub-part.
exam_structure.examA_vs_examB: Mo'ed A and Mo'ed B in the SAME semester are near-identical in structure, weighting, difficulty, and topic mix (both cover the full syllabus: OOP design + generics + Python). B is simply the second sitting with different themed scenarios, not an easier/harder or differently-scoped paper. So any past A or B is equally good practice; there is no 'A is theory, B is coding' style split. The only mild trend is that later-in-year sittings sometimes swap which patterns/containers appear (e.g. Composite in one, Decorator in the other).
exam_structure.time_pressure_notes: 3 hours for ~250-350 lines of correct, const-correct, memory-safe C++ plus Python is tight. Q1 alone is ~half the exam and its sub-parts are dependent, so a wrong base-class decision cascades. Budget roughly 80 min on Q1, 55 min on Q2, 40 min on Q3, 5 min slack. Grab the cheap guaranteed points first: UML sketches, the 'name the pattern / does it need a copy ctor / requirements on T' theory parts, and the Git question are fast and high-yield. Do not perfect one subclass while leaving whole sub-parts blank.

practice_bank.question: Given an abstract base Contestant (holds name, pure-virtual print(ostream&), and a free operator<< that calls print), implement an intermediate SingleAct (adds country, age) and its subclasses Singer and Dancer (Dancer adds a DanceType). Make 'cout << singer' print 'Jimmy, Singer, 23 years old from Canada' and 'cout << dancer' print 'Efrat, Tango Dancer, 25 years old from UK'.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Factor shared state into an intermediate base and delegate only the varying text to a virtual hook (getTitle) while one print() owns formatting. Reinforces base-ctor calls in the init list, override, and a virtual destructor on the base.

practice_bank.question: Matrix<T> stores its elements in a raw T* allocated with new T[n*m]. Does it need a user-defined copy constructor? If so, implement it exception-safely.
practice_bank.teaching_note: A class owning a raw pointer needs the Rule of 3: the default copy is a shallow pointer copy => double-free/aliasing. Drills deep copy and cleanup-and-rethrow exception safety in a constructor.

practice_bank.question: Write encrypt_char(c, k): Caesar-shift a single-character string c by k. Leave non-letters unchanged, preserve case, wrap cyclically. Allowed: ord, chr, isalpha, isupper, lower. Avoid the magic number 26.
practice_bank.solution:
def encrypt_char(c, k):
    if not c.isalpha():
        return c
    low = c.lower()
    size = ord('z') - ord('a') + 1
    out = chr((ord(low) - ord('a') + k) % size + ord('a'))
    return out.upper() if c.isupper() else out
practice_bank.teaching_note: Modular arithmetic for a cyclic shift; derive the alphabet size as ord('z')-ord('a')+1 rather than hardcoding 26; preserve case by remembering isupper().

practice_bank.question: Implement a generic HashSet<T, HashFunctionType>. Elements live in 'size' buckets; a user-supplied hash object maps an element to a bucket index. Implement HashSet(int size, HashFunctionType hash), contains, insert (no-op if present), remove (no-op if absent). Throw HashOutOfRange if a computed index is outside [0,size). std::set/std::map are forbidden.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Class template with a FUNCTOR type parameter, vector<list<T>> bucket design, bounds-checked exceptions, and STL count / list::remove. (The official key's range check used m_hash.size() by mistake; it should be m_table.size().)

practice_bank.question: For the HashSet<T> above: (b) what must type T support? (c) Does HashSet need a copy ctor, assignment operator, or destructor? Justify.
practice_bank.solution: (b) T needs operator== (for contains/dedup/remove), a copy constructor (elements are copied in on insert), and a destructor. (c) No user-defined copy ctor/assignment/destructor are needed: all dynamic memory is owned by the STL members (vector, list), whose compiler-generated copy/assign/destroy recurse correctly. You would only need the Rule of 3 if the class held a raw owning pointer.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Two skills: enumerating a template's implicit requirements on T by scanning the operations you call, and articulating exactly when the Rule of 3 does NOT apply (STL members => defaults are correct).

practice_bank.question: A Tea can take multiple toppings (e.g. Jasmine halves its bitterness), possibly several of the same kind, applied in any order, and extensible to future toppings. Name the design pattern and implement the abstract TeaTopping, a concrete Jasmine, and Tea::addTopping.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Choosing a pattern from the requirements AND justifying it against the trap alternative. Also drills virtual destructor, a const pure-virtual signature, and override.

practice_bank.question: Without using the re module: (a) starts_with_address(words) returns the length (in words) of an address prefix - a number, then 1-2 street-name words, then one of 'Street'/'Avenue'/'Road' - or None. (c) find_killer(line, context) returns each address in the line surrounded by `context` words on each side, clamped at the line edges, using comprehensions only (no loops).
practice_bank.teaching_note: str.isdigit / any / slicing, and boundary clamping with max(0, ...) (forgetting the clamp is the flagged common mistake). Replacing loops with comprehensions.

practice_bank.question: Implement template<class V> class Graph for an undirected simple graph (no self-edges, no duplicate vertices/edges; an edge's endpoints must already be vertices). Provide addVertex, containsVertex, addEdge(a,b), containsEdge(a,b) all returning bool, then operator+= (merge another graph) and a free operator+. Edge<V> with a symmetric operator== is given.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Template class design plus the canonical idiom of implementing operator+ as a free function on top of member operator+=. V must support copy and operator==.

practice_bank.topic: BST template: copy ctor + virtual override pitfall
practice_bank.question: Given template<class T> class Tree with a nested Node{ shared_ptr<Node> left,right; T data; }, root, and a provided insert_aux: implement insert and find, write a copy constructor, state what T must support, and implement TreeUnique (rejects duplicates). What changes if raw Node* replaces shared_ptr?
practice_bank.teaching_note: Deep copy of a recursive structure, the 'calling a virtual from a constructor' hazard, and how removing smart pointers reintroduces the Rule of 3.

practice_bank.question: Implement class Ship built from a unique_ptr<Warrior> captain (owned exclusively by the ship). addSailor(shared_ptr<Sailor>) adds crew but throws ShipIsFull past 12. canSail() returns true only if at least 5 crew are Warriors, else throws TooFewWarriors. Crew are shared (a sailor may serve several ships). Ships must not be copyable.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Choosing unique_ptr vs shared_ptr by ownership model, deleting copy ops when a class owns a unique resource, dynamic_cast for runtime type discrimination, and custom exceptions.

practice_bank.question: Write a template function groupBy that takes a set<T> and a callable f: T -> int, partitions elements into groups where equal f(elem) land together, and returns set<set<T>>.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Deducing a functor as a template type parameter and using a map as an index to bucket data. A follow-up reuses it with a lambda [mod](int x){return x%mod;} and throws DivideByZero on mod==0 - layering lambdas + exceptions on a generic helper.

practice_bank.question: Given a Logger with a private constructor, make a single global instance reachable everywhere and write a main that logs two messages. Then: should Logger be copyable/assignable? Justify and show the code.
practice_bank.teaching_note: The Meyers-singleton idiom (function-static for lazy init + automatic teardown) and why a singleton must delete its copy/assignment operations.

practice_bank.question: Define an abstract Action with pure-virtual execute(). Implement PrintAction (prints a stored string) and BeepAction (calls a global beep()). Then add a MacroAction that holds several sub-actions and, on execute, runs them all in order.
practice_bank.teaching_note: The Composite pattern: an Action composed of Actions, with a virtual interface, virtual destructor, and unique_ptr ownership of the children.

practice_bank.topic: Rule of 3 without STL (raw memory pool)
practice_bank.question: Implement template<class T> class MemManager for a real-time system (no STL, no smart pointers). The ctor takes numBlocks and blockSize, allocates numBlocks*blockSize T values in ONE array (throw invalid_argument for non-positive params). acquire() returns a pointer to a free block or throws NoMoreBlocks; release(const T*) frees a block. Also: are the default copy ctor/assignment sufficient? Explain.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Rule of 3 with raw arrays, exception-safe construction (cleanup + rethrow), pointer arithmetic for block indexing, custom exceptions, and articulating why shallow copy is unsafe.

practice_bank.question: Vegetable is an abstract base (weight, basePrice, virtual getPrice()); Tomato doubles the price when weight < 20g. Implement Cart holding vector<unique_ptr<Vegetable>>, with addVegetable(name, weight, basePrice) that creates a Cabbage or Tomato by name (throw UnsupportedVegetable otherwise) and getTotalPrice(). Explain why a virtual clone() is needed instead of a copy ctor, add clone(), and write Cart's copy constructor.
practice_bank.teaching_note: The virtual-clone / Prototype idiom, deep-copying a container of owning pointers, polymorphic pricing, a name-based Factory, and custom exceptions - a very representative hard Q1.

practice_bank.question: (a) merge_lists(a,b) merges two ascending lists into one sorted list in O(n), no built-in sort. (b) reduce(init,l,f) folds f across l from init. (c) merge_lists_in_path(path) reads a file whose lines are space-separated ascending ints and returns one merged sorted list using no loops (comprehensions), propagating any open error.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Two-pointer linear merge, implementing/using a fold (reduce) as a higher-order function, nested comprehensions replacing loops, and letting file exceptions propagate.

practice_bank.question: Book is now abstract (virtual getPrice() const, virtual getTitle() const). Upgrades change the price and are order-dependent (a special cover adds 8 shekels). Name the pattern and implement BookWithCover, which wraps a Book and adds 8 to its price.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Decorator = composition over inheritance: wrap an owning smart pointer to the wrapped object and augment a computed value. Order-dependence is the tell that distinguishes it from a flat subclass.

practice_bank.topic: templated ring buffer + Rule of 3 reasoning
practice_bank.question: Implement generic bounded CyclicBuffer<T> (no STL). Capacity fixed at construction. push adds at the tail, pop removes at the head; throw FullBuffer on overflow and EmptyBuffer on underflow. Implement ctor/dtor/push/pop/size, and explain whether a copy ctor and assignment are needed.
practice_bank.teaching_note: The ring-buffer trick (one spare slot so full != empty, modulo for wraparound giving O(1) ops) plus the Rule of 3 reasoning for a class that owns raw memory.

practice_bank.question: Extend CyclicBuffer<T> so that 'for (auto x : buffer) cout << x;' works as a read-only traversal from head to tail.
practice_bank.teaching_note: What a range-based for expands to (begin/end + != + prefix ++ + *), a nested iterator holding a reference back to its container, and reusing the container's modulo advance.

practice_bank.question: Implement an Area storing animals by unique name: addAnimal(unique_ptr<Animal>), hasAnimal(name), removeAnimal(name) returning the removed animal, getTotalWaterConsumption(). Then extend so an area can also contain other areas (addArea(unique_ptr<Area>)) and name the pattern.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Composite with recursive delegation through the hierarchy, transferring unique_ptr ownership with std::move, and knowing which methods must be virtual for the recursion to dispatch correctly.

practice_bank.question: Implement template<class T> class cow_ptr holding T* data and size_t* ref_count. Provide the explicit ctor, copy ctor, copy assignment, destructor (correct ref-count teardown), const T& operator*() const, T& operator*() (which performs copy-on-write via a private detach()), and size_t count() const.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Rule of 3 with a shared reference count, copy-on-write triggered only by the non-const accessor, self-assignment safety, and providing both const and non-const operator*. A variation on the shared_ptr shown in tutorials.

practice_bank.question: (a) Implement generic all_of(first,last,pred). (b) Implement calcAvg(first,last,func) = mean of func over the range (throw on empty). (c) Using them and std::sort, write calcAreas(vector<shared_ptr<Shape>>, x) = the average area of the x largest shapes.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Iterator-range template algorithms that work over any container, passing predicates/lambdas, composing your own generic functions, and slicing the top-x tail after sorting with a comparator.

practice_bank.question: Given books = [("1984",328),("Hobbit",304),("Dune",688),("Hamlet",342)], a loop groups UPPERCASED titles by shelf number (pages-1)//200 + 1 into a dict of lists. Reproduce the same result using comprehensions only (no loops, no recursion).
practice_bank.teaching_note: The two-pass group-by comprehension idiom: first build the set of distinct keys, then a nested list-comprehension per key. Avoids imperative accumulation entirely.

practice_bank.question: (d) Give the git commands to stage all changes, commit with a message, and push the current branch to origin. (e) Explain what a merge conflict is and what the markers <<<<<<< / ======= / >>>>>>> mean, and how you resolve it.
practice_bank.solution:
(d)
  git add .
  git commit -m "your message"
  git push origin <branch-name>
(e) A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same lines and git cannot auto-merge. Git writes both versions into the file: the current branch's version between '<<<<<<< HEAD' and '=======', and the incoming branch's version between '=======' and '>>>>>>> <branch>'. You resolve it by editing the file to keep the desired content, deleting the three marker lines, then git add the file and commit.
practice_bank.teaching_note: Low-effort, high-certainty points: memorize the add/commit/push sequence and the three conflict markers. Rebase can produce up to one conflict per replayed commit; a merge produces at most one.
